data.title: Blackstone to build $1.2B West Virginia gas power plant
data.description:
Blackstone (BX) confirmed plans Thursday to invest $1.2 billion to build a 600 MW natural gas power generation facility in Harrison County, West Virginia, to help meet rising electricity demand from AI data centers and other energy-intensive industries.

The Wolf Summit project, which would be the first-ever combined-cycle natural gas power plant built in West Virginia, will supply power to Old Dominion Electric Cooperative, which serves ~1.5 million residents across Virginia, Maryland and Delaware, the company said.

Wolf Summit adds to Blackstone's (BX) recent U.S. energy investments, including the Hill Top Energy Center in Pennsylvania and the Potomac Energy Center in Virginia; the firm said its Blackstone Energy Transition Partners unit has backed ~1,600 MW of new power generation in the U.S. during the past three-and-a-half years.

"Helping meet the rising demand for electricity from AI and other areas is among our highest conviction investment themes," Blackstone (BX) senior managing director Bilal Khan said.

data.title: IRS boosts contribution limits for 401(k)s and IRAs in 2026
data.description:
The Internal Revenue Service unveiled on Thursday new limits for contributions people can make to their retirement savings plans in 2026.

The 401((k)) limit increased by $1,000 to $24,500 for next year, while the limit on annual contributions to an IRA (individual retirement account) increased to $7,500 from $7,000.

The IRA catch-up contribution limit for individuals who are at least 50 years old was amended to include an annual cost-of-living adjustment to $1,100 from $1,000 in 2025, the IRS said.

The catch-up contribution limit for 401((k)), governmental 457 plans, and the federal government's Thrift Savings Plan rose to $8,000 for 2026, from $7,500 for 2025. Under a change made in SECURE 2.0 legislation, employees who are aged 60 to 63 can make a higher catch-up contribution. That limit remains at $11,250.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Michael Burry closes company, names Phil Clifton as successor
data.description:
"Big Short" investor Michael Burry has reportedly closed his firm Scion Asset Management and named Phil Clifton as his successor. 

Earlier in the day, several posts on X circulated a photo of a letter dated October 27 from Burry to Scion's investors saying that he was winding down his fund. 

The closure of the firm was confirmed by the Financial Times, citing two people with direct knowledge of the letter Burry sent to investors.

"With a heavy heart, I will liquidate the funds and return capital—but for a small audit/tax holdback – by year’s end. My estimation of value in securities is not now, and has not been for some time, in sync with the markets," the letter said.

In the same letter, he added, "I do suggest investors contact my associate PM Phil Clifton regarding his coming endeavors."

Earlier in the day, Burry's Scion Asset Management terminated its registration status with the Securities and Exchange Commission, effective November 10.

Last week, Burry’s Scion Asset Management took bearish positions in AI darlings Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR), as he hinted at caution toward the artificial intelligence trade that has boosted a lot of this year's market rally.

Burry, who had famously predicted the 2008 financial crisis, has been going after big tech lately. He has recently been vocal about potential risks in big tech and AI valuations. On Tuesday, he sounded a new alarm and said that major cloud and AI infrastructure companies were artificially inflating profits by extending the “useful life” of their assets.

Burry had shut his previous company, Scion Capital, in 2008. He then reopened it in 2013 as Scion Asset Management. 

Click here to check out a timeline on Burry's bold market plays over the years

More related stories

  • Michael Burry accuses tech giants of inflating profits by stretching asset lifespans
  • Palantir CEO Alex Karp slams ‘Big Short’ investor Burry for betting against Palantir, Nvidia
  • Michael Burry adds bullish bets on Pfizer, Halliburton, Molina, and Lululemon
  • Michael Burry’s ‘Big Short’ instincts target AI trade with puts on Nvidia, Palantir

data.title: How power bottlenecks could slow the U.S. in the AI race against China – GS
data.description:
Power supply constraints may become a critical factor limiting U.S. dominance in artificial intelligence, according to Goldman Sachs.

The U.S. currently leads the global data center market with 44% of worldwide capacity, but this advantage may be eroding due to power limitations, said Hongcen Wei, commodities strategist in Global Investment Research at Goldman Sachs.

According to the strategist, “Eight out of the 13 U.S. regional power markets are already at or below critical spare capacity levels,” while U.S. peak summer effective spare power generation capacity has decreased significantly from 26% five years ago to 19%, approaching the industry’s 15% critical threshold.

This power market tightness has already triggered significant price spikes in the U.S. electricity market, particularly in the PJM region, which includes Virginia, “the world’s data center capital.”

Data centers currently drive approximately 6% of U.S. power demand, with projections indicating this could rise to 11% by 2030, further straining an already tight grid system.

In stark contrast, China is rapidly expanding its power infrastructure across multiple sources. By 2030, China is projected to have “effective power spare capacity equivalent to over three times the world’s expected data center power demand (~400 GW vs. ~120 GW),” Wei said.

 

 

 

He identified three key factors contributing to U.S. power market tightening: data center-driven demand growth, insufficient capacity building to offset coal retirements, and limited deployment of power storage technologies.

“Limited effective spare power capacity could act as a bottleneck for further data center developments in the U.S.,” Wei warned, noting that while “U.S. policy efforts could help,” they are “unlikely to reverse the tightening trend in the next few years” due to long development timelines and supply chain constraints.

Artificial Intelligence/Robotics ETFs: (AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (GINN), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), (CHAT)

Data center ETFs: (DTCR), (CLOU), (BLOK)

data.title: Chicago Atlantic signals continued portfolio growth with $98M liquidity and fifth consecutive $0.34 dividend
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Chicago Atlantic BDC, Inc. (LIEN) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Peter Sack highlighted Chicago Atlantic BDC's unique positioning as "the only BDC focused on and able to lend to cannabis companies, together with a focus on the lower middle market commonly underserved by capital providers." Sack reported net investment income per share of $0.42 for the third quarter and stated, "We executed on our pipeline and funded $66.7 million to 13 new investments, of which 7 were new borrowers, which improved diversification of the portfolio and allowed us to utilize our credit facility. I'm proud to say that's a new originations record for us."
  • Sack emphasized differentiation from industry issues, noting the company's "weighted average yield on debt investments as of September 30, 2025, was 15.8% compared to 11.4% for the average BDC" and explained, "99.5% of our portfolio is senior secured compared to other BDCs who have an average of 19.5% exposure to subordinated debt, equity and JV investments."
  • He announced a $0.34 dividend, "marking the fifth consecutive quarter at that rate. This dividend is also well covered this quarter with net investment income per share of $0.42."
  • Interim CFO Thomas Geoffroy stated, "Financial highlights for the third quarter were gross investment income totaling $15.1 million compared to $13.1 million for the second quarter. Interest income included $1.9 million of onetime prepayment and make-whole fees from unscheduled repayments. Net investment income was $9.5 million or $0.42 per share, up from $7.7 million or $0.34 per share in the second quarter."
  • President Bernardino Colonna described Q3 as a record for originations, with $66.3 million in new debt investments and seven new borrowers, noting "100% of them are senior secured and 84% are either fixed or floating rate loans at their respective floors as of quarter end."

Outlook

  • Management plans to "focus on further diversifying the portfolio, utilizing the credit facility and managing interest rate sensitivity while maintaining the overall strength of the portfolio." Sack indicated that the company "will maintain our high bar when it comes to underwriting and structuring investments that deliver above-market risk-adjusted returns."
  • Colonna reported the pipeline as of quarter end included "approximately $610 million in potential debt transactions," and stated, "We expect additional deployment activity between now and year-end, but at a more measured pace than the robust gross originations activity we have seen in the last 2 quarters."

Financial Results

  • Gross investment income for Q3 was $15.1 million, with net expenses of $5.6 million. Net investment income reached $9.5 million or $0.42 per share. Net assets totaled $302.9 million, and net asset value per share was $13.27 at quarter end. The company reported $97.8 million in liquidity, including $92.5 million of borrowing capacity and $5.3 million in cash.
  • The portfolio consisted of 37 company investments, with 24% in non-cannabis companies and an average credit investment size of approximately 2.4% of the debt portfolio. There was $11 million of debt outstanding from the credit facility.
  • Loan repayments and amortization totaled approximately $62.7 million, including $59.6 million in early principal payoffs. Unfunded commitments stood at approximately $27 million.

Q&A

  • Pablo Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates: "Were there some repayments that were unexpected? And did that, in a way, force you to be more aggressive in lending in the quarter to show some book growth, if you can comment on that?" CEO Sack responded, "while we had a larger amount of repayments in Q3 than expected, that does not impact the pace of our deployment because our pipeline and deployment is simply not that reactive to liquidity for better or worse."
  • Zuanic inquired about the outlook for the cannabis industry with recent regulatory changes. Sack replied, "the closing of the hemp-related loophole, I suspect is going to support state regulated markets and be a positive catalyst for most of our borrowers."
  • On tax liabilities, Zuanic asked about companies negotiating uncertain tax provisions. Sack explained, "we assume that uncertain tax liabilities, unpaid tax liabilities, we view them as indebtedness that ultimately will have to be paid. When and under what terms is difficult to forecast, but we view it in no uncertain terms as a liability and obligation of our borrowers."
  • Zuanic questioned the risk of lending to smaller, midsized companies. Sack stated, "with smaller companies, we also have more leverage and bargaining power and negotiate with greater assets of downside protection in our loans."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were inquisitive and focused on repayment trends, industry outlook, and borrower risk, but their tone remained neutral to slightly positive, with congratulations on deployment and an interest in understanding risk management.
  • Management’s sentiment was confident and emphasized differentiation, discipline, and risk controls, with CEO Sack stating, "I'm proud to say that's a new originations record for us," and repeatedly highlighting the company's proactive approach.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained a constructive tone, though the current quarter featured more probing on industry risk and repayment dynamics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize measured deployment activity for the rest of the year, compared to a strong pipeline expectation in the previous quarter.
  • There was an increase in portfolio diversification, originations, and non-cannabis exposure, with new originations and net investment income both reaching records.
  • The focus of analysts shifted slightly from macro trends and regulatory uncertainty in Q2 to risk management and repayment impact in Q3.
  • Management maintained a confident tone but was more explicit about risk controls and origination discipline in the current quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified the potential impact of unexpected loan repayments but stated that deployment pacing is not reactive to liquidity changes.
  • Interest rate sensitivity remains a focus, with the company "better positioning the company against the drop in interest rates" and high interest rate floors mitigating risk.
  • Tax liabilities for borrowers were described as an ongoing risk, with Sack stating these are "a liability and obligation of our borrowers."
  • No non-accruals were reported in the portfolio, and underwriting remains disciplined to mitigate credit risk.

Final Takeaway

Chicago Atlantic BDC emphasized record portfolio originations, a well-covered dividend, and disciplined risk management while maintaining a strong liquidity position and a differentiated lending strategy focused on underserved markets. Management signaled continued measured growth and solid credit performance, with a robust pipeline supporting further deployment for the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Compass Point starts coverage on CoreWeave with bullish rating
data.description:
Compass Point started coverage on CoreWeave (CRWV) with a Buy rating, saying that a boost in AI-driven data center spending and secured contracts from blue-chip clients strengthened conviction that the AI infrastructure provider’s growth is durable.

The brokerage set a PT of $150 on the stock, implying an upside of over 75% to the stock’s last close.

Analyst Michael Donovan said that the rising tide of investment in AI infrastructure provides a massive tailwind for CoreWeave and the company “is positioned to ride that wave as a leader in GPU cloud computing.”

“Though a relatively new public company—listed March 28, 2025 —CoreWeave already serves Microsoft and has captured long-term demand: revenue backlog reached $55.6B at the end of 3Q25, up 85% sequentially from $30.1B in 2Q25; backlog consists of $50.0B remaining performance obligations plus $5.6B of additional amounts expected under committed contracts, supported by clients like OpenAI with $22.4B committed and Meta with a $14.2B deal through 2031. Nvidia, a 7% shareholder, agreed to a $6.3B capacity backstop to ensure any unused GPUs continue to generate revenue through 2032,” Donovan noted.

CoreWeave was trading 5% lower on Thursday in afternoon trading. However, the stock has more than doubled in value so far this year, compared to the over 16% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street are bullish and rated the stock a Buy.

Earlier on Wednesday, CoreWeave signed an agreement to increase its revolving credit facility to $2.5B from $1.5B and extend the maturity date to November 2029 from May 2028.

CoreWeave reported its fiscal third-quarter results on Monday, which saw revenue surge 132.9% year-over-year to $1.36 billion.

data.title: ReNew to spend $9.3B on green energy projects in India's Andhra Pradesh state
data.description:
India's ReNew Energy (RNW) said Thursday it plans to spend ~820 billion rupees ($9.33 billion) in the state of Andhra Pradesh to expand its clean energy portfolio, as the country pushes to double its non-fossil fuel power generation capacity to 500 GW.

ReNew (RNW) said it will set up a 6 GW photovoltaic ingot-wafer plant, a 2 GW pumped hydro project, a 300,000 metric tons/year green ammonia facility, and 5 GW of hybrid renewable projects in the state, including wind-solar and solar-plus-storage.

The latest investment commitment includes 600 billion rupees in new investments and adds to the 220 billion rupees pledged in May for a hybrid renewable energy project, the company said, without describing how it will fund the projects.

ReNew (RNW), which already operates 717 MW of wind and 60 MW of solar capacity in Andhra Pradesh, said it aims to develop 78.5 GW of solar, 35 GW of wind and 25 GWh of battery storage capacity in the state.

data.title: From the 'Big Short' to the big mystery: A timeline of Michael Burry’s bold market plays
data.description:
Nearly two decades after he became a Wall Street legend for predicting the 2008 housing crash, investor Michael Burry once again came out of the shadows with his bold bets against AI darlings Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR).

However, in a surprise move, Burry's firm, Scion Asset Management, has deregistered with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, effective November 10.

Here’s how one of the market’s most notorious contrarians got here—and why investors are watching closely: 

2004–2008: The “Big Short” era

Burry forayed into the big leagues in the mid-2000s by spotting the rot in America's housing market years before it collapsed. He had launched his hedge fund, Scion Capital, in November 2000. 

During the mid-2000s, through Scion Capital, he used credit-default swaps to bet against subprime mortgage bonds.

A report from Vanity Fair suggested that by the second quarter of 2005, Scion Capital had amassed over $1 billion in CDS exposure on subprime mortgage bonds, a bet against what Burry believed was a market waiting to implode.

His bet paid off massively as the market imploded in 2007-2008.

Burry had made his mark. Michael Lewis chronicled the story in his book The Big Short: Inside the Doomsday Machine. The book was later made into a movie named The Big Short, where actor Christian Bale played Burry. 

However, after the euphoria settled, Burry shut Scion Capital and took a break from managing other people's money.

Reports suggested he shut down the company in 2008, as he was flooded by lawsuits and IRS audits.

2013–2020: The return 

In 2013, Burry came back and reopened his company, this time in the name of Scion Asset Management. 

This time, his concern was about what he called the “index bubble.” In a 2019 interview with Bloomberg News, he compared the rise of index funds to the pre-2008 boom in subprime collateralized debt obligations (CDOs). He added passive investing has removed price discovery from the equity markets.

Burry had also reportedly built a huge position in GameStop (GME) before it became a massive meme stock and surged leaps and bounds. However, he had sold his entire stake in late 2020, thus missing out on the 2000% leap that GME saw at one point in 2021. 

2021: The Tesla gamble

In May 2021, filings revealed that Scion had taken large put-option positions against Tesla (TSLA), reflecting Burry’s belief that the EV maker’s valuation had become detached from fundamentals. A Reuters report said the company had put options on 800,100 shares in the Elon Musk-founded company as of the end of the first quarter in 2021.

Regarding GME's meteoric rise in 2021, Burry had reportedly tweeted in January, saying that the squeeze was “unnatural, insane and dangerous,” and called for legal and regulatory consequences for such trading. By this time, Burry had sold his position in GME. 

2025: The AI warnings

Burry, once again, seemed to keep a low profile. However, in the past few weeks, he returned to the limelight with his bold views on AI and tech companies. 

He also changed his profile name on X to Cassandra Unchained. Cassandra was a Trojan princess whose accurate predictions were doomed to be disbelieved.

October 31, 2025

Burry shared a photo of actor Christian Bale as Burry in a scene from the movie “The Big Short” and wrote, “Sometimes we see bubbles. Sometimes, there is something to do about it. Sometimes, the only winning move is not to play.”

His posts prior to October 31 appeared to be deleted.

November 4, 2025

Last week, Burry’s Scion Asset Management took bearish positions in AI darlings Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR), as he hinted at caution toward the artificial intelligence trade that has boosted a lot of this year's market rally. He also disclosed new bullish positions in Pfizer (PFE), Halliburton (HAL), Molina Healthcare (MOH), and Lululemon Athletica (LULU).

Soon enough, Palantir (PLTR) CEO Alex Karp hit back and criticized short sellers while calling out Burry. “The two companies he’s shorting are the ones making all the money, which is super weird,” Karp told CNBC. “The idea that chips and ontology are what you want to short is bats--- crazy,” Karp said.

November 10, 2025

However, that did not change Burry's views. On Tuesday, Burry sounded a new alarm, targeting Big Tech's accounting practices. He said that major cloud and AI infrastructure companies are artificially inflating profits by extending the “useful life” of their assets. He had named big companies, including Oracle (ORCL), Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN) in a post on X.

In the same post, he said more details will be revealed on November 25.

His current X bio reads, “Official X account for 'The Big Short'. Michael Burry, M.D., dubbed 'Cassandra' by Warren Buffett. Now unchained—??? launches Nov 25th, Stay Tuned!”

November 13, 2025

An SEC filing and a post on Burry's X feed confirmed that Scion Asset Management has terminated its registration status with the Securities and Exchange Commission, effective November 10.

Several posts on X circulated a photo of a letter dated October 27 from Burry to Scion's investors saying that he was winding down his fund.

November 25, 2025

In his latest post, Burry once again alluded to the November 25 date and said, “On to much better things November 25.”

Burry has offered no further clarification on what “November 25” signifies, leaving investors guessing.

More related stories

  • Michael Burry accuses tech giants of inflating profits by stretching asset lifespans
  • Palantir CEO Alex Karp slams ‘Big Short’ investor Burry for betting against Palantir, Nvidia
  • Michael Burry adds bullish bets on Pfizer, Halliburton, Molina, and Lululemon
  • Michael Burry’s ‘Big Short’ instincts target AI trade with puts on Nvidia, Palantir
